name: HMS Unseen (P 51)
events: The history of HMS P 51 / Unseen as compiled on this page is extracted the from patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in July 2015.
1 Jul 1942 P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
[Lt. Crawford had been Wanklyn’s First Lieutenant on HMS Upholder.]
[No logbooks are available of HMS P 51 for the months of July and August 1942. Details for these two months will thus be missing.] (1)
2 Jul 1942 P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training.
At Holy Loch she was officially taken over from the shipyard having successfully completed her acceptance trials. (1)

27 Jul 1942 Around 0930 B/27, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick. She made the passage together with HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack). (1)
29 Jul 1942 Around 0745 B/29, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Lerwick. After taking on board fuel and fresh provisions she proceeded on her 1st war patrol around 1900 B/29.
This was a work-up / anti U-boat patrol in the Norwegian Sea in area 'K 102' between 61°30'N and 62°20'N and 00°30'E and 00°30'W.
En route to the patrol area she was to proceed through position 60°38'N, 00°01'W.
For the daily positions of HMS P 51 during this patrol see the map below.

30 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 0951 B/30, the Capt. S.9 sent the following information to HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN); Total bombing restriction arranged 35 miles either side Greenwich meridian between 63°30'N and 61°20'N till further notice. (3)
5 Aug 1942 While surfacing for a sun sight, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), damaged 'Q' tank due to an error in drill by the diving panel watchkeeper.
Despite the damage / leaks, HMS P 51 was able to remain on patrol.

In a signal timed 1503 B/5, HMS P 51 informed the Capt. S.3 of the following; Leak developing between Q tank and forward battery tank caused by blow from Q tank with Kingston valve shut. Intend remaining on patrol. Full outfit of torpedoes remaining. (4)

6 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1137 B/6, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, RN); 1) Leave patrol P.M./8 and proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through position 090° - Muckle Flugga - 10 miles to Lerwick adjusting speeds to make your E.T.A. as follows, P 51 at 0900 A/9 and P 247 at 1500 A/9. 2) Total bombing restrictions will be arranged for the whole passage.

In a follow up signal timed 1745 B/6, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 51; Return to Lerwick forthwith through position 91° - Muckle Flugga - 9 miles. [This was due to the fact that time needed to be gained as P 51 was to be docked for repairs to the damaged Q tank.] (4)
7 Aug 1942 At 0800 A/7, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick.
She departed for Scapa Flow, escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR), later the same day [orders were to sail at 2330 B/7]. (1)
8 Aug 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow where the damage to 'Q' tank was inspected.
It was decided that the submarine should proceed to the Chatham Dockyard for repairs. (1)
11 Aug 1942 Around 0900 B/11, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Methill where she was to join convoy FS 80 for onward passage to Chatham.
During the passage from Scapa Flow to Methill, HMS P 51 was escorted by HMS Leicester City (T/Lt. C.E. Irving, RNVR). (1)
12 Aug 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived off Methill from Scapa Flow. (1)
13 Aug 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Methill for Sheerness. She made the passage in convoy FS 80, 9th series.
[For more info on this convoy see the 'event', 'Convoy FS 80 / 9th series' for 13 August 1942.] (1)
This convoy departed Methil around 1330 B/13.
It was made up of the following merchant vessels; Agatha (Dutch (tanker), 3369 GRT, built 1927) and Don (British, 1037 GRT, built 1924).
The submarine HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) and the motor minesweeper HM MMS 191 (T/Lt. H.A.G. Ceulemans, RNR) were also part of the convoy.

On departure from Methil the convoy was escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Liddesdale (Lt.Cdr. A.A. Mackenzie, RNR) and HMS Lauderdale (Lt. G.D. Pound, DSC, RN). The A/S trawler HMS Berkshire (T/Skr. W. Patterson, RNR) was also with the convoy with the task to provide close escort for HMS P 51.
Off 22 buoy a merchant vessel joined the convoy. This was the Lochside II (British, 368 GRT, built 1925). She was later detached to the Tyne. [This ship is not mentioned in the Commodore report.]

From Blyth the following merchant vessels joined the convoy; Betswood (British, 1051 GRT, built 1936), Corglen (British, 2822 GRT, built 1928), Corstar (British, 2337 GRT, built 1918), Corundum (British, 929 GRT, 1925), Crackshot (British, 2379 GRT, built 1924), Hoogland (Dutch, 1298 GRT, built 1916) and Vilk (British (former Estonian), 1933 GRT, built 1913).
At 0830 B/14, the convoy overtook and merged with the Tyne section of the convoy off 18C buoy.

The Tyne section of the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bramhill (British, 1821 GRT, built 1923), Bruce M. (British, 1887 GRT, built 1927), Fulham VI (British, 1552, built 1941) and Stanhope (British, 2337 GRT, built 1919).
The Tyne section was escorted by the auxiliary AA vessel HMS Mona's Isle (Cdr.(Retd.) J.H. Blair, DSO, RD, RNR) which returned to the Tyne after the convoy sections had merged.

From Sunderland the following merchant vessels joined the convoy; Capitol (British, 1558 GRT, built 1941), Cerne (British, 1242 GRT, built 1924), Corfirth (British, 1803 GRT, built 1934), Ewell (British, 1334 GRT, built 1926), Ightham (British, 1337 GRT, built 1914), Penshurst (British, 1454 GRT, built 1921), Redcar (British, 1475 GRT, built 1920), Redriff (British, 1547 GRT, built 1925) and Sylvia Beale (British, 1040 GRT, built 1938).

According to the convoy escort report a merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from the Humber. This was the tanker San Roberto (British (tanker), 5890 GRT, built 1922) which passed Spurn Head on 14 August and arrived at Harwich on 15 August. [This ship is not mentioned in the Commodore report.]
At 1440 B/14, off 62D buoy, the A/S trawler HMS Lady Philomena (Skr. A. Robinson, RNR) took over from HMS Berkshire as close escort for HMS P 51.
At 1700 B/14, off H3 buoy, the patrol vessel HMS Mallard (Lt.Cdr. D. Parsons, RD, RNR) and two ML's, HM ML 204 (T/Lt. G.C. Davey, RNVR) and HM 299 (Lt. D.A.P. Smith, RANVR) joined the convoy escort.

According to the convoy escort report a merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Harwich, this was the Southwick (British, 443 GRT, built 1917) and one merchant vessel was detached to Harwich, the San Roberto). Also HM MMS 191 was detached to Harwich.
The convoy was disbanded off Southend around 1200 A/15. (5)

15 Aug 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Sheerness. She then proceeded to the Chatham Dockyard where she was to be taken in hand for repairs to her damaged 'Q' and battery tanks. (1)
7 Sep 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) is undocked at the Chatham Dockyard.
[We currently have no data on when she was docked but presumably fairly quickly after her arrival at the Chatham Dockyard and de-ammunitioning.] (6)
15 Sep 1942 With her repairs completed, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), proceeded from the Chatham Dockyard to Sheerness. Before arrival at Sheerness D/G trials were carried out. (6)
16 Sep 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials off Sheerness. (6)

17 Sep 1942 Around 1345 A/17, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Sheerness for Portsmouth. She made the passage together with HMS Unbeaten (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN). They were escorted by the barrage balloon vessel HMS Bungay (T/Lt. G.D. Davies, RNR). (6)
18 Sep 1942 Around 1430 A/18, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Gosport (Portsmouth) from Sheerness.
Before putting into harbour D/F calibration trials were carried out. (6)
20 Sep 1942 At 1330 A/20, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar.
Before final departure independent exercises were carried out until 1645 A/20.

P 51 was routed through the following positions; 'A' = 50°33'N, 01°51'W, 'B' = 180° - Start Point - 25 miles, 'C' = 180° - Lizard Head - 30 miles, 'D' = 49°16'N, 09°00'W, 'E' = 49°09'N, 12°00'W, 'F' = 47°00'N, 12°10'W, 'G' = 45°00'N, 12°20'W and 'H' = 43°00'N, 12°30'W, E.T.A. 2000 A/27. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.)
For the daily positions of HMS P 51 during this passage see the map below.
22 Sep 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) proceeded to Falmouth to seek medical attention for the first lieutenant which was suffering from severe haemorrhage that could not be stopped.
P 51 was escorted in by HMS ML 179 (?)
Apparently after treatment, the first lieutenant (Lt. R.T. Sallis, RN), was able to rejoin the submarine as there was no replacement.

0335 A/22 - Successfully passed the signal timed 0135 A/22 which contained the following; First Lieutenant suffering haemorrhage. Intend returning to Falmouth for medical attention. Present position 49°29'N, 05°02'W approximately.

At 0610 A/22, the Capt. S.5's signal timed 0452 A/22 was received which contained the following; Your 0125 A/22. Rendezvous with escort in position 180° - Lizard Head - 5 miles at 0700 A/22. Total bombing restriction have been arranged.
In a follow up signal the Capt. S.5 sent the following information to HMS P 51; My signal timed 0452 A/22. Escort will be ML 179. A PW convoy is to be expected at D.1 buoy at 0800 A/22.
In a signal timed 0640 A/22, HMS P 51 sent the following to the Capt. S.5; E.T.A. at the rendezvous will be at 0830 A/22.
0820 A/22 - Made rendezvous with ML 179 and proceeded in company to Falmouth.
1005 A/22 - Arrived at Falmouth where the first lieutenant was landed for treatment. (8)
23 Sep 1942 At 1545 A/23, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) resumed her passage to Gibraltar. She was escorted out of Falmouth by HMS ML 186 (?) and HMS ML 259 (?) until 1825 A/23.
Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). [We have been unable to locate a signal regarding the further routing of HMS P 51.] (7)
30 Sep 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) reported to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) that she had encountered heavy weather and that she was delayed. (8)
4 Oct 1942 Around 1245 A/4, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from the U.K. (8)
12 Oct 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises off Gibraltar. (9)
13 Oct 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar with HMS Verity (Lt.Cdr. R. Horncastle, RN). (9)
14 Oct 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar with HMS Verity (Lt.Cdr. R. Horncastle, RN).
On completion of these exercises, HMS P 51 carried out gunnery exercises. (9)

16 Oct 1942 At 1245 A/16, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was to perform a work-up patrol in the Alboran Sea (north of Alboran Island).
Before departing for patrol exercises were carried out with HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN).
[No patrol report is available for this patrol. Fortunately the logbook for October is available.]
17 Oct 1942 At 0132 A/17, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area near Malaga, Spain.
At 0250 A/17, a neutral merchant vessel was sighted bearing 300° at a range of 6.8 miles.
At 0400 A/17, a trawler was sighted bearing 345° at a range of 7 miles.
At 0500 A/17, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 210° at a range of 4.5 miles.
At 0624 A/17, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 0930 A/17, a neutral tanker was sighted bearing 251° at a range of 5 miles. A practice attack was made on this tanker. The exercise was completed half an hour later.
At 1500 A/17, a merchant vessel was seen bearing 270° at a range of 4.5 miles.
At 1945 A/17, HMS P 51 surfaced.
At 2230 A/17, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 250° at a range of 5.5 miles. (9)

18 Oct 1942 At 0240 A/18, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted neutral merchant vessel on which a dummy surface attack was carried out. On completion of this attack a gunnery exercise (dummy) was carried out. Exercises were completed at 0300 A/18.
At 0600 A/18, HMS P 51 dived.
At 0720 A/18, a sailing vessel was seen bearing 150° at a range of 4.5 miles.
At 0945 A/18, a tanker was seen bearing 130°.
At 1000 A/18, a dummy submerged attack was carried out on this tanker which was seen to be Spanish.
At 1130 A/18, a dummy submerged attack was carried out on a Vichy-French moerchant vessel.
At 1815 A/18, a dummy submerged attack was carried out on a Spanish merchant vessel.
At 1950 A/18, HMS P 51 surfaced. (9)
19 Oct 1942 At 0350 A/19, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted merchant vessel bearing 340° at a range of 8.5 miles. A dummy surfaced attack was carried out.
At 0600 A/19, a dummy surfaced attack was carried out on a merchant vessel that was sighted on bearing 268° at a range of 7 miles.
At 0643 A/19, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 1907 A/19, HMS P 51 surfaced.
At 2000 A/19, a lighted merchant vessel was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 8 miles. A dummy surfaced attack was carried out.
At 2028 A/19, the dummy attack was completed and a second attack was now started on a merchant vessel seen bearing 205° at a range of 5 miles. (9)
20 Oct 1942 At 0240 A/20, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted merchant vessel bearing 160° at a range of 8 miles.
0450 A/20, a dummy surface attack was carried out on a neutral merchant vessel seen bearing 240° at a range of 5 miles.
At 0640 A/20, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 0945 A/20, a Spanish trawler was seen bearing 003° at a range of 4.5 miles.
At 1000 A/20, a merchant vessel was seen bearing 055° at a range of 6 miles. A submerged dummy attack was carried out.
At 1232 A/20, a neutral merchant vessel was seen bearing 064° at a range of 5.7 miles. A submerged dummy attack was carried out.
At 1948 A/20, HMS P 51 surfaced. (9)
21 Oct 1942 At 0400 A/20, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a neutral merchant vessel bearing 098° at a range of 9 miles. A surface dummy attack was carried out.
At 0604 A/20, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 0910 A/20, a Spanish merchant vessel was seen bearing 261° at a range of 8 miles. A submerged dummy attack was carried out.
At 1955 A/20, HMS P 51 surfaced. (9)
22 Oct 1942 At 0645 A/22, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived for the day.
At 1330 A/22, a neutral merchant vessel was seen bearing 030° at a range of 7 miles. A submerged dummy attack was carried out.
At 1950 A/22, HMS P 51 surfaced. (9)
23 Oct 1942 At 0200 A/23, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) left her patrol area to return to Gibraltar.
She was to pass through position 36°20'N, 04°10'W at 0500 A/23 and arrive at Gibraltar at 1200 A/23. She was not to cross 04°30'W before 0700 A/23.
At 0830 A/23, two Italian repatriation ships were sighted proceeding westwards.
At 0950 A/23, HMS P 51 dived for a dummy submerged attack on these two Italian vessels. The attack was completed at 1015 A/23.
[The Italian vessels sighted were the Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) and Saturnia (23940 GRT, built 1927). They were en-route to former Italian East Africa.
Around 1200 A/23, HMS P 51 ended her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (10)

1 Nov 1942 At 1600 A/1, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Toulon, Southern France, taking the place of HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) which had developed telemotor problems with her after hydroplanes and was returning to Gibraltar.
2 Nov 1942 At 1058 A/2, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a Spanish trawler bearing 340° at a range of 6.5 miles.
At 1732 A/2, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 155° at a range of 9 miles. (11)
3 Nov 1942 At 0615 A/3, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a darkened ship bearing 335° at a range of 5 miles.
At 1000 A/3, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 060° at a range of 5 miles.
At 1400 A/3, a trawler was sighted bearing 020° at a range of 4.5 miles. (11)

4 Nov 1942 At 2238 A/4, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted neutral merchant vessel bearing 035° at a range of 8.5 miles. Within the hour two more lighted neutral merchant vessels were sighted and evaded.
This were most likely Vichy French merchant vessels. [Note the patrol report gives the time of sighting these vessels as 0230 A/5. It also states their courses were approximately 200°.] (11)

5 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1835 A/5, the Capt. S. 8 sent the following orders to the submarines listed; 1) HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN) is to proceed to waiting a position in the vicinity of position 43°01'N, 05°01'E. 2) HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) is to patrol in area 'A'. (12)

7 Nov 1942 At 2255 A/7, to the south of Toulon, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 320° proceeding on a course of 040° at 15 knots. P 51 then withdrew to the southward to report this destroyer in accordance with her patrol orders.
[We have been unable to identify this obviously Vichy-French warship.]
At 0230 A/8, after having retired to the southward, a signal timed 2258 A/7 was passed which reported the destroyer.
At 0315 A/8, course was set to return to the patrol area. (8)

8 Nov 1942 At 0830 A/8, to the south of Toulon, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted an Elan-class minesweeping sloop bearing 020° proceeding on a course of 215° at 10 knots. This vessel remained in the area throughout the day patrolling to the westward of P 51. (8)
9 Nov 1942 At 0520 A/9, to the south of Toulon, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) had to dive early on sighting a small darkened vessel. No HE was picked up.
At 1405 A/9, a trawler came out of the harbour and patrolled to the eastward of P 51 while transmitting on Asdic for a short period.
At 1410 A/9, an Elan-class minesweeping sloop was seen patrolling to the westward. (8)
10 Nov 1942 At 0750 A/9, to the south of Toulon, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a trawler patrolling to the eastward.
Two Elan-class minesweeping sloops were seen patrolling in the area throughout the day, one to the westward and one to the eastward. No A/S transmissions were heard. (8)

11 Nov 1942 At 0007 A/11, to the south of Toulon, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted an Elan-class minesweeping sloop bearing 070° at a range of 1.8 miles proceeding on a course of 270°. P 51 turned towards.
At 0010 A/11, P 51 dived and took avoiding action. Asdic transmissions were heard.
At 0126 A/11, P 51 surfaced and continued patrol.
At 0545 A/11, P 51 dived for the day.
At 0600 A/11, HE was picked up bearing 020°. The source of the HE was stopping and starting probably hunting with the use of hydrophones.
At 0645 A/11, HE was picked up bearing 285°. Three minutes later the bridge and mast of an Elan-class minesweeping sloop were sighted. This vessel patrolled to the eastward of P 51 all day.
At 1300 A/11, an Elan-class minesweeping sloop was seen coming out of the harbour. Half an hour later this vessel commenced to patrol to the westwards of P 51.
Between 1745 A/11 and 1900 A/11, distant explosions were heard.
At 1908 A/11, HMS P 51 surfaced and retired to the south to keep clear of the Vichy-French A/S patrols. (13)
12 Nov 1942 At 0430 A/12, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) commenced to close Toulon Bay.
At 0545 A/12, the submarine dived for the day.
At 0900 A/12, a trawler was seen patrolling east and westwards on about latitude 43'00'N. This trawler was transmitting with her Asdic occasionally.
At 1250 A/12, a patrol vessel was seen bearing 350° at a range of 6000 yards. The course of this vessel was 090°.
At 1305 A/12, the patrol vessel altered course 180°.
At 1330 A/12, the patrol vessel altered course 330°.
It was suspected the French may be aware of a submarine being in the area.
At 1730 A/12, HMS P 51 commenced to retire to the southward.
At 1800 A/12, HE was multiple patrolling A/S vessels was heard.
At 1930 A/12, HMS P 51 surfaced. (8)
13 Nov 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) is detected and depth charged by a Vichy French patrol vessel about 10 nautical miles south-west of Toulon, France in position 42°58'N, 05°49'E.
0545 A/13 - Dived for the day.
0558 A/13 - Picked up HE from a patrol vessel bearing 030°.
0645 A/13 - Sighted an Elan-class minesweeping sloop. The Vichy ship was closing the signal station and was soon lost from sight.
0800 A/13 - The same vessel was returning and came straight towards P 51.
0825 A/13 - Went deep. The Vichy vessel passed down the starboard side at a range of about 1000 yards.
0839 A/13 - The vessel decreased speed and five splashes were heard similar to laying mines.
0840 A/13 to 0845 A/13 - The Vichy vessel crossed astern and moved up the port side.
0852 A/13 - The vessel again decreased speed and five more splashes were heard.
0900 A/13 - HE faded. Came to periscope depth. The Elan-class vessel was sighted proceeding on the usual patrol to the westward.
1039 A/13 - Heard four loud but distant explosions.
1500 A/13 - Sighted the Vichy French 'Elan'-class patrol vessel / minesweeper that had been sighted earlier in the day returning to our area. Position was 42°58'N, 05°49'E.
1520 A/13 - The Vichy vessel crossed our bow at a range of 2000 yards.
1530 A/13 - The vessel altered course to the northward.
1550 A/13 - The vessel was now proceeding at slow speed at a range of only 400 yards. Went deep immediately.

1551 A/13 - Five depth charges were dropped very close. P 51 was taken to 120 feet but could not be held at that depth. 'Q' tank had been damaged and was now full of water as was 'H' tank. Unseen was sinking fast and was finally held at 345 feet by blowing no.1 and no.6 main ballast. 'Q' tank was damaged, the outboard vent had been blown open by the depth charges.
1555 A/13 - six more depth charges were dropped also rather close. Fortunately no more depth charges were dropped after these.
1955 A/13 - Surfaced and withdrew to the southward.

2331 A/13 - Passed our signal timed 1504 Z/13 which contained the following; Destroyer patrolling in my area since arrival. Depth charges dropped very close today, not seriously damaged am withdrawing for 24 hours to the vicinity of position 42° 40'N, 05°50.E. [A weather report was also included in the signal]. (8)
14 Nov 1942 At 0545 A/14, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived for the day and patrolled near position 42°40'N, 05°50'E.
At 1200 A/14, course was set for area 'C'.
At 1700 A/14, HMS P 51 entered area C.
At 1900 A/14, the submarine surfaced.

At 2010 A/14, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1310 Z/14 was received and five minutes later course was altered to 170° to leave patrol. [For the text of the signal see the other 'event' for 14 November 1942.] (8)

14 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1310 Z/14 the Capt. S.8 sent the following information / orders to the submarines mentioned in the signal; 1) Toulon patrol is to be discontinued. 2) At 0001 Z/15 submarines are to proceed diving by day as follows; HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) direct to pass position 'X' (43°50'N, 08°01'E) at 1800 Z/15. HMS Sturgeon (Lt. D.J. Beckley, RN) through position 'Y' (41°41'N, 04°04'E) at 0001 Z/15th thence pass position 'X' at 1800 Z/16. HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) through position 'Z' (39°19'N, 00°01'E at 0001 Z/15, thence to pass position 'X' at 2300 Z/17. 3) P 228, Sturgeon and Tribune will be routed by the Capt. S.10 after passing position 'X' coastwise via Genoa to establish patrol off Naples where Italian main units now are. Object attack enemy shipping but one full salvo is to be retained for use off Naples. 4) HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) are to leave patrol diving by day so as to pass through position 'P' (42°01'N, 05°55'E at 0430 A/15 and 0600 A/15 respectively and position 'Q' (39°15'N, 00°28'E) at 0530 A/17th and 0001 A/18th respectively. Onward route to Gibraltar will follow. (12)

15 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1650 Z/15 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; Onward route dived by day. 1) HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) from position 'Q' proceed 5 miles off Spanish coast through position 'R' (135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles at 0030 Z/18, Position 'S' 180° - Cape de Gata - 5 miles at 2000 Z/18 and arriving at Gibraltar 1000 Z/19. 2) HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) proceed by same route through position 'R' at 0100 Z/19 then adjust speed to pass position 'S' at 1200 Z/20 and arrive at Gibraltar at 1000 Z/21. 3) Both submarines proceed on surface at discretion of the Commanding Officer on the day of arrival. (12)

16 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1004 Z/16, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following; Keep a good lookout for U-boat. No British submarine your vicinity.
This may have referred to U-380 which had been patrolling near the Spanish coast in the area the British submarines were to pass through. U-380 however left that area that very same day. (14)

18 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0950 Z/18, the Capt. S.8 informed HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following; A convoy for Algiers and Oran is leaving Gibraltar at 2200 Z/18 through positions 36°15'N, 04°33'W, 36°25'N, 02°51'W and 36°02'N, 01° 27'W and may be encountered.
This referred to convoy TE 5. (14)
21 Nov 1942 At 1100 Z/21, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (8)
24 Nov 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at the Gibraltar Dockyard for repairs to 'Q' tank. (11)
26 Nov 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) is undocked. (11)
4 Dec 1942 At 1530 A/4, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for passage to Malta.
The submarine was heavily laden with spare gear and additional personnel. Because of this she was not be used operationally except in an emergency.

6 Dec 1942 At 0210 A/6, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), sighted a what was thought to be German U-Boat at a range of 1000 yards. Position was 37°24'N, 00°59'W. The U-Boat could not be attacked as the torpedo tubes were dry and there was not enough time to blow up the tubes. HMS P 51 dived to evade a possible attack by the enemy.

The submarine sighted was indeed a German U-Boat as it was U-561. The Germans had also sighted HMS P 51 at 0214 A/6 [according to the German KTB] but were also unable to attack as the British had most likely seen the Germans earlier and dived. The Germans then also dived. (15)

10 Dec 1942 At 0223 A/10, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) transmitted her signal timed 0158 A/10 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; No onward route received. Will remain in the vicinity of position 38°40'N, 08°01'E where I arrive at 0530 A/10.

At 0940 A/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0653 A/10 was received which contained the following; Your onward route through positions 38°30'N, 08°32'E and 38°30'N, 10°28'E thence as ordered in my signal 0005 A/1. Keep to your starboard of route 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) to 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E) passing 'J' to 'K' during daylight/12. Keep W/T silence in Sicilian channel except for signals of greatest urgency. Make rendezvous with the minesweeper at end of Malta searched channel at 1028 A/13. (16)

12 Dec 1942 At 1005 A/12, in position 37°00'N, 11°42'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted an armed tug which looked like the Italian Titano (788 GRT, built 1913) proceeding towards Pantelleria. (8)
13 Dec 1942 At 1245 A/13, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar.
She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined at 1030 A/13. (8)

21 Dec 1942 At 1415 A/21, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Skerki Bank, north of Tunis, Tunisia on the traffic lane between Bizerta and Palermo.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 51 during this patrol see the map below.
22 Dec 1942 At 0612 A/22, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived in position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).
At 1811 A/22, HMS P 51 surfaced.

At 2030 A/22, the signal 1902 A/22 from the Capt. S.10 was received which contained the following; At 1730 A/22, one merchant vessel and two destroyers in position 40°23'N, 14°03'E, course 222° at 13 knots. In anticipation of enemy preliminary sweeps you should remain east of position 'H' until necessary to gain patrol position by 0700 A/23.
Between 2100 A/22 and 0130 A/23, HMS P 51 patrolled in the vicinity of position 37°14'N, 11°18'E. (18)
23 Dec 1942 At 0130 A/23, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) left the vicinity of position 37°14'N, 11°18'E for the the patrol area.
At 0532 A/23, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 0600 A/23, HMS P 51 commenced to patrol within 10 miles of position 37°32'N, 10°40'E.
At 1132 A/23, in position 37°32'N, 10°40'E, three large destroyers were seen steering a course 0f 065° at 15 knots.
At 1215 A/23, in position 37°35'N, 10°43'E, a 5000 tons merchant vessel escorted by one destroyer was seen. An attack was commenced.
By 1235 A/23, it was evident that the target was going to pass out of range. The attack was abandoned.

At 1620 A/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1501 A/23 was received which contained the following; Keep south east of your present position until it is necessary to move to position 37°35'N, 10°43'E by daylight/24.
At 1830 A/23, HMS P 51 surfaced.

At 2257 A/23, in position 37°16.5'N, 10°51'E, HE was heard bearing 020°. As the night was very dark and visibility was under 1000 yards due to heavy rain, Lt. Crawford ordered P 51 to dive. The HE was thought to be originating from one or two destroyers proceeding westwards.
At 2330 A/23, the HE faded and HMS P 51 surfaced. Visibility was improving. (17)

24 Dec 1942 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) attacked a large escorted transport vessel with four torpedoes to the north of the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°32'N, 10°45'E. No hits were obtained.

The transport attacked was most likely the Italian transport Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyers Bombardiere and Camicia Nera. The attack was not observed. This convoy had departed Bizerta at 0630 A/24-0632 A/24 and arrived at Naples at 0317 A/25-0433 A/25.
0115 A/24 - Set course for to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°42'N, 10°43'E.
0548 A/24 - Sighted a small destroyer bearing 350° proceeding on course 200° at 18 knots. Dived to avoid being sighted.
0628 A/24 - In position 37°34'N, 10°44'E, heard HE of a destroyer bearing 350°. The enemy vessel was proceeding to the southwards making 240 revolutions.

1000 A/24 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0605 A/24, which contained the following; At 0510 A/24, three merchant ships and four destroyers in position 38°12'N, 11°51'E. course 238°, speed 10 knots.
[Viminale, Col di Lana and KT 2 convoy ???]

1028 A/24 - In position 37°32'N, 10°45'E sighted what is thought to be the German transport Ankara escorted by two, possibly three destroyers bearing 260°, range about 5 nautical miles. Started attack.
1033 A/24 - Fired four torpedoes from 2500 yards.
1039 A/24 - Heard a loud explosion this was thought not to be an exploding torpedo. Withdrew to the south-eastwards. No counter attack followed.
1200 A/24 to 1600 A/24 - There was intense air activity in the area. Continuous explosions were heard which were believed to be aircraft bombs.
1850 A/24 - Surfaced. (8)
25 Dec 1942 At 0241 A/25, in position 37°36'N, 10°47'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted a small destroyer proceeding on course 040°, speed 20 knots. P 51 dived to avoid being sighted.
At 0312 A/25, on surfacing, HMS P 51 sighted a small destroyer laying stopped in position in position 37°36'N, 10°42'E. P 51 dived again. The destroyer subsequently moved off to the north-eastwards.
At 0340 A/25, HE was picked up bearing 040°.
At 0400 A/25, three destroyers or torpedo boats passed overhead doing 320 revs. They were proceeding to the south-west.
At 0500 A/25, HMS P 51 surfaced. Lt. Crawford decided to remain in the vicinity of P 51's current position in view of the traffic encountered.
At 0625 A/25, HMS P 51 dived for the day.
At 0856 A/25, in position 37°37'N, 10°40'E, smoke was sighted bearing 155°. P 51 closed at speed.
At 0905 A/25, the masts and funnels of two merchant vessels were seen. The attack was abandoned as P 51 was already abaft their beam.
[XXI Aprile and Carlo Zeno convoy ?]
At 1200 A/25, smoke was sighted bearing 220°. The submarine altered course towards.

At 1230 A/25, in position 37°33'N, 10°48'E, three large destroyers were sighted proceeding on course 045° at 22 knots. One destroyer altered course towards the submarine. The enemy reduced speed for about 10 minutes but then rejoined the other two destroyers.
At 1830 A/25, P 51 surfaced.
At 1920 A/25, in position 37°34'N, 10°49'E, the HE of destroyers doing 23 knots was picked up on bearing 320°. Due to poor visibility due to rain nothing was sighted. P 51 dived.
At 1940 A/25, the HE faded out to the southward. P 51 surfaced. (17)
26 Dec 1942 At 1235 A/26, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0801 A/26. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 26 December 1942.]
At 1850 A/26, HMS P 51 set course for position 37°38'N, 10°42'E, so as to be down moon of the estimated track of the enemy convoy reported in the above signal. (8)
26 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0801 A/26, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) of the following;
At 0615 A/26 two small merchant ships and one escort to the north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°26'N, 12°28'E on course 240° at 5 knots. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (19)
27 Dec 1942 At 0325 A/27, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted three darkened ships in position 37°37'N, 10°45'E. Their course was 240. An attack was started.

The targets were however soon identified as being three large destroyers proceeding at 20 knots. As P 51 was on the wrong side of the moon and a close attack was essential for success in was decided to break off the attack and dive which was done at 0331 A/27.
At 0419 A/27, HMS P 51 surfaced.
At 0628 A/27, HMS P 51 dived for the day.

At 1400 A/27, in position 37°39'N, 10°48'E three large destroyers were seen proceeding on course 060° at 20 knots. It looks like these may be the same ones as seen during the night but now on their return journey.

The destroyers seen on both occasions were most likely the Italian destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Antonio Pigafetta and Legionario, which had sailed from Palermo at 2000 B/26-2030 B/26 and arrived at Tunis and La Goulette at 0730 B/27-0852 B/27. They had been carrying troops to Tunisia.
[Return journey ???]
At 1840 A/27, HMS P 51 surfaced. (8)
28 Dec 1942 While still operating north of the Gulf of Tunis, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), was detected and bombed by an enemy aircraft but she sustained no damage.

Later the same day and enemy convoy was attacked with three torpedoes but no hits were obtained. The target was most probably the Italian transport Caterina Costa (8060 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Bersagliere and Mitragliere, they had sailed from Bizerta for Palermo. The attack was unobserved.
According to the Italian naval archives, (Ufficio Storico della Marina Militare) this attack was on the Col di Lana/Viminale-convoy but this appears to be an error as they were farther north.
0402 A/28 - In position 37°30'N, 10°53'E sighted an aircraft approaching. Dived. The aircraft dropped three bombs that exploded moderately close but caused no damage to P 51.
0430 A/28 - Heard the HE of two destroyers / torpedo boats doing about 25 knots. The HE faded to the south-westward.
0545 A/28 - Surfaced.
0614 A/28 - Dived for the day.
1727 A/28 - In position 37°34'N, 10°39'E sighted a 4000 tons transport ship escorted by two destroyers. Enemy course 070°, estimated speed 11 knots. Commenced attack on the zig-zagging enemy.
1740 A/28 - Fired three torpedoes from 2000 yards. No hits were obtained and no counter attack followed.

2000 A/28 - Lt. Crawford decided to return to Malta as there was only one torpedo left. The chance of success with only one torpedo on the heavily escorted convoys was small. The enemy was also aware of the position of the submarine due to the attack and due to the fact that aircraft appeared to have detected the submarines three times last night.
2120 A/28 - Surfaced and set course for position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E). (8)

29 Dec 1942 At 0003 A/29, in position 37°24'N, 11°12'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) heard very loud HE bearing 160°. P 51 dived. The HE of three vessels with very high revolutions was picked up. It was thought this may be Allied motor torpedo boats en-route to Bone.
[HM MTB 265, HM MTB 266, HM MTB 307 and HM MTB 315 were on passage from Malta to Bone during this night. Submarines had been informed of this.]
At 0030 A/29, HMS P 51 surfaced.
At 0054 A/29, HMS P 51 was detected and bombed by an enemy aircraft in position 37°22'N, 11°15'E. Six bombs were dropped, rather close but P 51 was not damaged.

At 0235 A/29, a small merchant ship with a destroyer were observed. Ten minutes later, in 37°14'N, 11°23'E, P 51 dived and observed them through the periscope but at 0257 A/29 they opened fire. P 51 went to 150 feet. The destroyer dropped three depth charges without causing any damage and hunted the submarine until 0500 A/29 before moving away on bearing 065°.
At 0518 A/29, HMS P 51 surfaced to charge.
At 0616 A/29, HMS P 51 dived and commenced the passage though positions 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E) at 3.5 knots.
At 1055 A/29, in position 36°49'N, 11°42'E, an A/S trawler was sighted bearing 330° at a range of 6000 yards. Enemy course was 120°, obviously making for Pantelleria.
At 1840 A/29, HMS P 51 surfaced in position 36°24'B, 11°57'E and set course for Malta at 8 knots.
At 2329 A/29, HMS P 51 cleared her signal timed 2103 A/29 in which she reported to the Capt. S.10 that she was returning to Malta. (8)

30 Dec 1942 At 0110 A/30, in position 36°03'N, 13°07'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted what was thought to be a British T-class submarine with drums on the casing. This must have been either HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO and Bar, RN) or HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN).
At 1115 A/30, HMS P 51 ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She proceeded into harbour unescorted. (8)
8 Jan 1943 At 1330 A/8, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean).
She was routed through positions 35°22'N, 14°14'E and 34°40'N, 14°14'E thence operating in an area bounded by the line joining Tripoli to Kerkennah No.6 buoy.

11 Jan 1943 At 0825 A/11, near Zuara, in position 32°56'N, 12°12'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted the masts and funnel of a small vessel bearing 115° at a range of 6 miles. The target appeared similar to a gunboat (trawler-type) of the Palmaiola-class. The target had three landing craft and a motor A/S boat in company.
[Perhaps Docilitas (1246 GRT, built 1900) with MZ 706, MZ 711 and MZ 750. They arrived at Sfax around 1500 A/12.]
At 1300 A/11, in position 32°56'N, 12°12'E five westbound landing craft were sighted.
[Perhaps F 350, F 477, F 542, MZ 708 and MZ 734 which had departed Tripoli around 0400 A/11 ?. They arrived at Sfax around 1300 A/12.] (20)

12 Jan 1943 At 0030 A/12, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2250 A/11 which contained the following; Aircraft returning from patrol report having sighted seven small merchant ships anchored 3 miles outside Sfax harbour at 1520 A/11.
In a follow up signal timed 0937 A/12 the Capt. S.10 sent the following; My 2250 A/11. All are under 500 tons and include at least 4 F-boats. Not considered suitable targets.
At 0408 A/12, off Zuara, in position 32°59'N, 12°09'E, two westbound landing craft were sighted. P 51 dived in ordered to avoid compromising the submarines position.
At 0435 A/12, HMS P 51 surfaced.
At 0548 A/12, HMS P 51 dived for the day.

At 1600 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1358 A/12 was received which stated that considerable shipping was reported between Ras Turgueness (33°50'N, 11°02'E) and the Kerkennah No.8 Buoy (34°28'N, 10°58'E).
At 1855 A/12, HMS P 51 surfaced and set course for Ras Turgueness.

At 2100 A/12, north-east of Zuara, in position 33°13'N, 11°57'E, a darkened ship was sighted bearing 010°. P 51 closed to attack. The targets course was 140° at a speed of about 10 to 12 knots. The target was identified as being a small destroyer but looked very like a submarine.
At 2120 A/12, the enemy vessel turned towards. P 51 dived. A/S transmissions could be heard. The enemy carried out a short A/S hunt but dropped no depth charges.
At 2223 A/12, HMS P 51 surfaced and proceeded. (20)
14 Jan 1943 At 1855 A/14, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) surfaced after two days of blank patrol off Ras Turgueness. Course was now set for the Kerkennah No.8 Buoy. (8)
15 Jan 1943 At 0607 A/15, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived for the day.
At 0830 A/15, HMS P 51 sighted the Kerkennah No.8 Buoy (34°28'N, 10°58'E). Submerged patrol was commenced about 4 miles to the south of the Buoy.

At 1300 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 A/15 was received in which she was ordered to keep within 10 miles of the coast in the Gulf of Gabes as Allied surface forces would be operating in the area tonight.
At 1850 A/15, HMS P 51 surfaced and proceeded to patrol in position 34°27'N, 10°46'E to keep clear of Allied surface forces.
This referred to an anti-shipping sweep in the Kerkennah area by HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN). (21)

16 Jan 1943 At 0035 A/16, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2228 A/15 was received in which HMS P 51 was ordered to keep north of 34°00'N during the night of 16/17 January.
This referred to an anti-shipping sweeper by HMS Pakenham (Capt. E.B.K. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN).
At 0610 A/16, HMS P 51 dived for the day and patrolled about 4 miles south-west of the Kerkennah No.8 Buoy.

At 1630 A/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1502 A/16 was received which contained the following; Aircraft reported that at 1015 A/16th, one medium sized merchant Ship and two small Merchant Ships 40 miles due west Tripoli westbound at 8 to 10 knots. In view of the expected Allied destroyer sweep during the coming night Lt. Crawford decided to continue the patrol near the Kerkennah No.8 Buoy.
At 1850 A/16, HMS P 51 surfaced. (21)
17 Jan 1943 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Zenobia Martini (1454 GRT, built 1911) north of Gerba Island, Tunisia in position 33°55'N, 11°02'E.

Zenobia Martini was escorted by the submarine chaser Oriole and was en-route from Tripoli, Libya (departed around 2200 A/15) for Italy via Sfax, Tunisia. Oriole picked up forty-seven survivors (including thirteen British PoWs) and a corpse, three crew members and five passengers were killed or missing.
0300 A/17 - Set course for Ras Turgueness at 8 knots.
At 0610 A/17 - Dived and continued to close Ras Turgueness lighthouse.
0700 A/17 - Sighted smoke bearing 160°.
0715 A/17 - The bearing of the smoke moved to the westward. Closed at speed.
0810 A/17 - Sighted a 2500 tons merchant vessel escorted by a Crotone-class escort vessel / minelayer. Commenced attack.
0906 A/17 - In position 33°55'N, 11°02'E fired three torpedoes. Two torpedo explosions were heard.
0908 A/17 - P 51 hit bottom with 45 feet on the depth gauge. Got off the bottom and withdrew to seaward. The escort dropped 6 depth charges but these were not close at all.
1130 A/17 - The escort disappeared to the south-eastward. Altered course to close the position of the attack.
1220 A/17 - Sighted wreckage including a lifeboat, a raft, empty oil drums and empty petrol containers. Also sighted a corpse.
1230 A/17 - Altered course to 130° to clear the area.
1540 A/17 - Sighted the escort vessel returning from the south-eastward. She was making for Sfax.

1615 A/17 - In position 33°52'N, 11°08'E, sighted a three-masted schooner and a two-masted schooner hugging the coast near the lighthouse. Proceeded at speed to intercept them to the east of the lighthouse.
1700 A/17 - The schooners were lost from sight against the land.
1840 A/17 - Surfaced and proceeded into the Bay to the south of Aghir (33°45'N, 11°01'E).

2000 A/17 - Nothing sighted. The schooners must have anchored very deep in the bay. Lt. Crawford decided to proceed down the coast towards Zuara at 8 knots in the hope of intercepting them during daylight tomorrow. (8)
18 Jan 1943 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Sportivo (1598 GRT, built 1900) 5 nautical miles bearing 20 of Zuara, Libya in position 33°00'N, 12°08'E.

Sportivo was en-route from Tripoli, Libya to Trapani, Sicily and was in convoy together with the small Italian transport Amba Alagi (450 GRT, built 1932) and were escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope. She sank in two minutes and the escort vessel hunted the submarine, dropping all her thirty-six depth charges, while Amba Alagi proceeded unescorted. Calliope returned to pick up the survivors; none had been killed and only three were slightly wounded, and returned to Tripoli.

0001 A/18 - In position 33°24'N, 11°36'E, sighted three darkened ships bearing 170°. Closed to investigate. The targets were identified as a small trawler, a small merchant vessel and a landing craft. The merchant vessel was too small for a torpedo attack. P 51 dived to open the range and avoid being sighted.
0030 A/18 - Surfaced and proceeded towards Zuara.
0930 A/18 - In position 32°56'N, 12°10'E sighted three ships bearing 110°, enemy course 285°. Started attack.
0945 A/18 - The ships were identified as a Spica-class torpedo boat, a 2500 tons merchant vessel and a 400-500 tons motor tanker.

1014 A/18 - Fired three torpedoes from 1000 yards. One torpedo hit was heard. P 51 went to 60 feet. The torpedo boat started a counter attack. Thirty-five depth charges were counted but P 51 managed to escape undamaged.
1200 A/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1245 A/18 - Altered course to 300° to intercept a destroyer and schooner reported in an aircraft report timed 1123 A/18 before dark.

1310 A/18 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat with the petrol carrier that had previously been sighted. They were zig-zagging between courses of 300° to 340° at 7 knots. As the reload was not completed no attack was possible.

1600 A/18 - Sighted the two schooners seen yesterday evening bearing 150° to 180° very close inshore. Closed at speed, but it was not possible to close to decisive range. Lt. Crawford decided to withdraw to seaward to charge and intercept these two schooner off Zuara tomorrow should they proceed to Tripoli. (8)
19 Jan 1943 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with two torpedoes west of Tripoli, Libya. No hits were obtained.
0550 A/19 - East of Zuara, in position 32°57'N, 12°27'E, sighted a darkened ship bearing 135° and another bearing 180°. Dived to avoid being sighted. A/S transmissions were picked up from both ships.
0700 A/19 - All was quiet now. Came to periscope depth. Nothing inside. Commenced to close the coast.
1015 A/19 - In position 32°51'N, 12°28'E, sighted smoke bearing 288°.
1135 A/19 - Carried out a dummy attack on an eastbound Crotone-class escort vessel.

1630 A/19 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1112 A/19 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol as necessary to pass through positions 33°26'N, 12°38'E at 2006 A/19, 33°54'N, 14°14'E and thence to make rendezvous with a minesweeper at the end of the searched channel at 0830 A/21.
After decoding the signal course was set for the first position mentioned.

1850 A/19 - In position 32°57'N, 12°31'E sighted two ships bearing 115°. Enemy course was 270°. Started attack. The leading ship was identified as a Crotone-class patrol vessel / minelayer and the second vessel was a small funnel-aft type merchant vessel of about 800 - 1000 tons.
1939 A/19 - Fired two torpedoes from 1500 yards. No hits were obtained. The enemy's speed was most likely badly underestimated.

This must have been the Italian naval auxiliary water tanker Q 6 / Irma (Italian, 305 GRT, built 1908) escorted by a minesweeper which had departed Tripoli around 1300 A/19. Irma was later sunk by the destroyers (see below). [Note: Irma had the engine and thus also the funnel aft.]
2033 A/19 - Surfaced. Sighted five darkened ships scattered round the southern horizon. Withdrew at speed in view of the Allied destroyer sweep P 51 had been informed about.

This must have referred to the anti-shipping sweep by HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN). They decimated a convoy of small ships during the night.
Between 2030 A/19 and 2200 A/19 a large amount of AA fire was seen round the southern horizon. (22)

20 Jan 1943 At 0200 A/20, north-north-west of Tripoli, in position 33°31'N, 12°56'E, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers bearing 240° proceeding on course 035° at 14 knots. P 51 dived to let them pass.
At 0233 A/20, HMS P 51 surfaced and proceeded.

The destroyers sighted must have been HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN) returning to Malta from their anti-shipping sweep to the west of Tripoli. (8)

21 Jan 1943 At 0615 A/21, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted the destroyers HMS Pakenham (Capt. E.B.K. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) returning to Malta from operations.
However it was not HMS Pakenham that was deployed on operations during the night of 20/21 January together with HMS Nubian but HMS Jervis (Capt. A.F. Pugsley, RN).
At 0830 A/21, HMS P 51 made rendezvous with her escort to Malta, HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR).
At 1100 A/21, HMS P 51 ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

31 Jan 1943 At 0930 A/31, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the southern approaches to the Straits of Messina.
She was escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).

At 1145 A/31, she commenced A/S exercises with HMS Speedy and HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). Also two practice attacks were made on HMS Speedy. The exercises were completed at 1545 A/31 after which HMS P 51 proceeded on patrol. (8)
31 Jan 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 51 during her 6th war patrol see the map below.
2 Feb 1943 At 0520 A/2, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived when etimated to be 7 miles from the Italian coast between Cape dell'Armi and Cape Spartivento.
At 0700 A/2, it was seen that P 51 was actually 15 miles from the coast.
At 1130 A/2, a three-masted schooner was sighted proceeding eastwards.

At 1210 A/2, a 1500 tons ship was seen in tow of a tug. They were proceeding eastwards. As it was not possible to close within 5000 yards, Lt. Crawford decided not to compromise the submarines position.
[Perhaps the same ship torpedoed and sunk on the 4th ?]

At 1330 A/2, a vessel was seen which was thought to be the Italian submarine chaser Albatros proceeding on course 280° and transmitting on Asdic. [This could not have been Albatros as this vessel had been sunk on 27 September 1941.]
At 1500 A/2, P 51 commenced an attack on a small eastbound vessel. The attack was abandoned when it was seen that the vessel was only about 300 tons and in ballast.
At 1700 A/2, P 51 withdrew from the coast to charge.
At 1912 A/2, HMS P 51 surfaced. (8)

3 Feb 1943 At 0100 A/3, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0003 A/3 which contained the following orders; Proceed with despatch to investigate position 39°20'N, 17°09'E where aircraft reported a tanker beached after torpedo attack at 2300 A/2.
P 51 was 140 miles from the reported position. Course was set to comply at full surfaced speed.
At 0521 A/3, HMS P 51 dived.
At 1245 A/3, in approximate position 38°13'N, 16°32'E, the masts and funnels of a small southbound vessel were seen close inshore.
At 1635 A/3, just south of Punta Stilo a three-masted schooner was seen proceeding northwards. This vessel was hugging the coast.
At 1845 A/3, HMS P 51 surfaced. In the evening the submarine was harassed by aircraft. (23)

4 Feb 1943 HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Le Tre Marie (1086 GRT, built 1925) about 6 nautical miles south of Punta Alice, Italy in position 39°16'N, 17°11'E. She had been on passage from Crotone to Taranto towed by the tug Costante (Italian, 294 GRT, built 1916)and was hit in the stern. She sank in a few minutes and the tug rescued the whole crew, only one was slightly wounded.
0539 A/4 - Dived for the day and closed the land at 3 knots.

1020 A/4 - In position 39°20'N, 17°17'E, sighted a two-masted A/S schooner bearing 230° at a range of 4 miles. Two small auxiliary minesweepers bearing 250° at a range of 4 miles. Their course was 360°.
At 1130 A/4, the two auxiliary minesweepers reversed course.

At 1200 A/4, HMS P 51 closed to position 170° - Alice Point - 2 miles and then proceeded southwards down the coast keeping one mile from the shore. There was no sign of the beached ship reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0003 A/3.
1557 A/4 - In position 39°16'N, 17°11'E sighted a 1500 tons merchant vessel being towed by a tug bearing 170°, speed 5 nautical miles. Started attack from inshore.
1633 A/4 - Fired two torpedoes from 1500 yards. One hit the target which appeared to sink in 5 minutes. The tug slipped its tow and was later seen towing a lifeboat towards the shore.
1645 A/4 - Withdrew to seaward and reloaded. (8)
5 Feb 1943 At 0600 A/5, while between Crotone and Punta Alice, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), heard the HE of two vessels on bearings 280° and 320°.
At 0630 A/5, a camouflaged motor torpedo boat and an A/S schooner were sighted.
At 0800 A/5, in position 38°11'N, 17°18'E, a two-masted and a three-masted schooner were sighted bearing 270° at a range of 4 miles. Their course was 180°. These are thought to be A/S schooners.
At 0900 A/5, in position 39°11'N, 17°14'E, two auxiliary minesweepers were sighted proceeding on course 360°.
At 1120 A/5, in position 39°14'N, 17°11'E, two camouflaged motor torpedo boats were sighted. They were northbound.

At 1553 A/5, the two A/S schooners seen at 0800 hours returned from the southward with two small camouflaged escort vessels. When they had passed P 51 they altered course towards. Lt. Crawford took his submarine to 80 feet to withdraw. The vessels appeared to proceed in circles round the submarine as if they were carrying out a hydrophone hunt. After an hour they left as their HE faded. On coming to periscope depth only the two schooners could be seen close inshore.
At 2150 A/5, HMS P 51 dived for a motor torpedo boat which was patrolling to the east of the submarine.
At 2300 A/5, HMS P 51 surfaced. All was clear. (8)

6 Feb 1943 At 1000 A/6, to the southward of Alice Point, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) sighted two A/S schooners, proceeding northwards one mile inshore of HMS P 51 which was patrolling about three miles from the shore.
At 1100 A/6, the masts of an A/S schooner to the northward were sighted. An aircraft was patrolling in the vicinity of this schooner. (8)
7 Feb 1943 At 0700 A/7, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol close to the north-east of Crotone. Three merchant vessels of about 1000 to 2000 tons were seen in the harbour.
At 0715 A/7, two A/S schooners were proceeding northwards along longitude 17°13'E.
At 1200 A/7, an armed trawler left the southern harbour of Crotone and proceeded to the northward keeping close inshore.
At 1600 A/7, the two A/S schooners seen in the morning were seen returning from the northward. (8)
8 Feb 1943 At 0630 A/8, near Crotone, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) heard the HE of two vessels bearing 220° and 250° which turned out to be two small escort vessels.
At 0720 A/8, these escort vessels proceeded northwards in line abreast about 5 cables apart.
At 0800 A/8, two auxiliary minesweepers left Crotone harbour and followed up astern of the escort vessels.
At 0820 A/8, two more auxiliary minesweepers left Crotone harbour and also proceeded northwards.
At 1415 A/8, the two escort vessels followed by the auxiliary minesweepers were seen returning from the north. All subsequently entered harbour.

At 1300 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1103 A/8 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 51; After dark tonight 8th Leave patrol so as to proceed through positions 38°20'N, 17°37'E, 35°30'N, 15°41'E, 35°20'N, 15°00'E, 35°20'N, 14°19'E to make rendezvous with a minesweeper at position '6' at 0830 A/11.
At 2035 A/8, HMS P 51 surfaced and left patrol in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1103 A/8. (23)

10 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1322 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN); Do not cross 14°30'E before dawn/11 owing to movements of surface forces. Then proceed on surface at your discretion to the end of the searched channel at 1300 A/11 and enter unescorted. (24)
11 Feb 1943 At 1600 A/11, HMS P 51 (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
[Apparently she was not escorted in.] (8)
21 Feb 1943 At 1300 A/21, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Hammamet.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unseen during this patrol see the map below.
22 Feb 1943 At 0528 A/22, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) dived an patrolled on the Lampedusa - Pantelleria route.
At 0815 A/22, north-north-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°41'N, 12°29'E, a ship was sighted bearing 190° at a range of 5 miles. Closed at speed.
The ship was subsequently seen to be a hospital ship of about 2000 tons. The range could not be closed to less than 3 miles. The hospital ship's course was approximately 330° at 13 to 14 knots.
At 0845 A/22, a serious leak was discovered. An attempt to make repairs was unsuccessful. A second leak was also discovered and it was decided to return to Malta for repairs after dark.
At 1900 A/22, HMS Unseen surfaced and set course for Malta.

At 2130 A/22, HMS Unseen cleared her signal timed 2005 A/28 which contained the following; Returning by reverse of outward route to arrive at the rendezvous at 0830 A/23. Ship's side caulking under padded piece of port main inlet leaking. (8)
23 Feb 1943 At 1030 A/23, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta for repairs.
She was escorted in by a minesweeper (not identified) which had joined at 0830 A/23. (8)
27 Feb 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta to resume her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was now ordered to patrol off Sousse, Tunisia. (8)
4 Mar 1943 While patrolling off Soussa, Tunisia HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) destroyed the wreck of the German merchant Macedonia (2875 GRT, built 1922).

Macedonia had been torpedoed and damaged by HMS P 35 on 13 December 1942. Macedonia had then been beached by her crew to prevent her from sinking. An air attack on 15 December 1942 had damaged her even further. Her cargo was being salvaged when Unseen attacked. A salvage crane was alongside when Unseen destroyed the wreck. There were no casualties.
(All times are zone -1) 1215 hours - Sighted a floating crane alongside a wreck apparently salving cargo. Started closing to attack.
1435 hours - In position 35°52'N, 10°39'E fired one torpedo which hit the wreck. Unfortunately the crane did not appear to have been damaged. She immediately cast off and returned to the harbour. (8)

6 Mar 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) fired a torpedo at a merchant vessel inside Sousse Harbour but the torpedo hit the breakwater instead. The south mole suffered some damage near a 75mm German battery. The patrol boats VAS 203 and VAS 206 sailed to hunt the submarine.

(All times are zone -1) 1046 hours - In position 35°51'N, 10°44'E sighted a ship bearing 040°, range about 5 nautical miles. Enemy course 270°, speed 8 to 9 knots. Started attack. The target turned out to be a trawler proceeding towards the harbour. Decided to fire a torpedo at the merchant vessel inside the harbour as the boom would be open to let this trawler enter the harbour.
1152 hours - In position 35°49'5"N, 10°42'E fired one torpedo at the centre of the entrance. Range was 5000 yards.
1155 hours - The torpedo exploded on the end of the breakwater just to starboard of the entrance. (8)
12 Mar 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
22 Mar 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the North of Sicily.

24 Mar 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) fired four torpedoes against the Italian merchant Saluzzo (3747 GRT, former French Tamara, built 1937) that was in a convoy about 30 nautical miles west-south-west of Isola Marettimo, Italy in position 37°52'N, 11°27'E. A torpedo explosion was heard at the correct interval but in fact no hits were obtained.

The convoy attacked was made up of the above mentioned Saluzzo as well as the Italian merchant Nicolo Tommaseo (4573 GRT, built 1943). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Libra, Perseo and Tifone.

(All times are zone -1) 0925 hours - In position 37°52'N, 11°27'E sighted a convoy of two merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers and aircraft. Started attack. The merchant vessels were of about 4000 and 3500 tons. Enemy course was about 050°, speed 12 - 12.5 knots.
1011 hours - Fired four torpedoes at the 3500 tons merchant vessel from 5000 yards.

1015 hours - Heard one explosion at about the correct interval. One of the destroyers then counter attacked but only four depth charges were dropped of which only two were close but these caused no damage.
1115 hours - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. (8)
3 Apr 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
14 Apr 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily.

16 Apr 1943 At 0245 hours (zone -2), in position 37°32'N, 11°49'E, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), was bombed by what was thought to be a Wellington aircraft. The bomb was dropped about 2 cables on the port beam but fortunately caused no damage. (8)

18 Apr 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ-2205 (1168 GRT, former French Le Jacques Coeur, built 1933) north-north-west of Isola di Femmine, Sicily, Italy in position 38°15'N, 13°13'E.

The convoy attacked by Unseen was made up of the Italian tanker Bivona (1646 GRT, former French Socombel, built 1943) escorted by the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2203 and the above mentioned UJ 2205 as well as the Italian patrol boats VAS 207 and VAS 230. UJ 2205 was hit in the engine room and sank at 1145 hours. Four had been killed and eleven seriously wounded, the Italian VAS boats came alongside to rescue the survivors while UJ 2203 hunted the submarine. Bivona was forced to return to Palermo and sailed again the next day only to be sunk by HMS Unrivalled.
(All times are zone -2) 1038 hours - In position 38°16'N, 13°11'E sighted three ships in line abreast escorted by two E-boats and two or more flying boats. Started attack.
1103 hours - Fired four torpedoes at a small tanker from a range of 800 yards. One hit was obtained and HE ceased immediately.

1110 to 1400 hours - The two E-boats started a counter attack. Depth charges were dropped singly. By 1200 hours 11 depth charges had been dropped. By 1400 hours a further 20 charges had been dropped. (8)
26 Apr 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
27 May 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform several special operations (beach reconnaissances).
The time at Malta between this and her previous patrol had been used for trials with Chariot 'human torpedoes'.
For the daily positions of HMS Unseen during this patrol see the map below.

29 May 1943 During the night of 29-30 May 1943, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), launched a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. Position in which the folbot was launched was 36°44'N, 14°34'E. They used a folbot as there was too high a swell to use a Chariot. The party left at 2200/29 and returned at 0200/30. Course was then set to return to Malta (all times zone -2). (8)
30 May 1943 At 1100 hours (zone -2) HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) returned to Malta. She departed again at 1800 hours. (8)

31 May 1943 During the night of 31 May - 1 June 1943, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), launched a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. Position in which the folbot and Chariot (the first time this was used in this capacity) were launched was 36°44'N, 14°34'E. The party left at 2218/31 and returned at 0230/01. Course was then set to return to Malta (all times zone -2). (8)
1 Jun 1943 At 1800 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), returned to Malta. (8)
2 Jun 1943 In the evening, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), after having embarked a new Chariot, departed Malta again for another beach reconnaissance. (8)

4 Jun 1943 At 1745 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), returned to Malta. She had been unable to launch the COPP party for another beach reconnaissance due to the bad weather. (8)
5 Jun 1943 At 1900 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), departed Malta again for another beach reconnaissance. (8)

6 Jun 1943 During the night of 6 - 7 June 1943, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), launched a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. Position in which the folbot and Chariot were launched was 36°44'N, 14°34'E. The party left at 2222/06 and returned at 0155/07. Course was then set to return to Malta (all times zone -2). (8)

7 Jun 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. A total of four beach reconnaissances had been undertaken, however one unfortunately could not take place due to bad weather. (8)

24 Jun 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform several special operations (beach reconnaissances, COPP VI) near Licata, Sicily. She had on board a party from COPP VI.

25 Jun 1943 At 2210 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. Unfortunately the mission had to be cancelled due to the poor visibility. (8)

26 Jun 1943 At 2200 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. The folbot with the party left at 2215/26 and returned at 0200/27. According to the leader of the party a second sortie was required on this beach. (8)

27 Jun 1943 At 2200 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. The folbot with the party left shortly after 2200/27 and returned at 0150/28. (8)

28 Jun 1943 At 2200 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. The folbot with the party left shortly after 2200/28 and returned at 0200/29. (8)

30 Jun 1943 At 2155 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance. The folbot with the party left shortly after 2155/30 and returned at 0105/01. It had been intended to do this beach reconnaissance the previous night but visibility had been too low. (8)
1 Jul 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

6 Jul 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was to launch two folbots to act as marking positions near Marzanimi for the Husky landings (invasion of Sicily).
No daily positions of HMS Unseen are known for this patrol so no map can be displayed.
9 Jul 1943 At 2155 hours, the submarine launched the two folbots but one sank accidentally and the other managed to take off with some difficulty.
At 2300 hours, HMS Unseen began to act as a beacon for the invading forces.
10 Jul 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

24 Jul 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform several special operations (beach reconnaissances) on the east Calabrian coast. She had on board a party from COPP VI.

30 Jul 1943 At 2200 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance near Cape Colonna, Crotone, Italy. The folbot with the party left shortly after 2200/30 and returned at 0030/31.

1 Aug 1943 At 2140 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance near Cape Colonna, Crotone, Italy. The folbot with the party left at 2200/01 and returned at 0055/02. (8)

3 Aug 1943 At 2140 hours (zone -2), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), surfaced to launch a COPP party for a beach reconnaissance near Cape Rizzuto, Crotone, Italy. The folbot with the party left around 2200/03 and returned at 0040/04. (8)

4 Aug 1943 While on patrol off Cape Rizzuto, Calabria, Italy, HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) fires four torpedoes against what is thought to be an Italian Regolo class light cruiser. No hits were obtained.

The force attacked was made up of the Italian light cruisers Luigi Cadorna and Scipione Africano and the torpedo boats Lince and Saggitario and were on a mission to lay mines off Cape Rizzuto. Cadorna reported being missed by one torpedo on the port side.
(All times are zone -2) 0542 hours - In position 38°45'N, 17°10'E sighted three ships bearing 012°. They were difficult to see against the land. Started attack.
0547 hours - Identified the ships as one cruiser with a torpedo boat ahead and either a small cruiser or large destroyer astern.
0557 hours - In position 38°50'N, 17°09'E fired four torpedoes from yards. No hits were obtained. (8)
7 Aug 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
20 Aug 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Bari in the Southern Adriatic.

24 Aug 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel east of Bari, Italy with three torpedoes. No hits were obtained. Unseen was depth charged following this attack.

This was Hermada (4421 GRT, built 1907) escorted by the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz and proceeding from Corfu to Bari. Hermada reported missed by four (sic) torpedoes astern and the torpedo boat carried a counter attack.
(All times are zone -2) 1651 hours - Sighted a 2000 tons merchant vessel, escorted by one 'Cosenz'-class torpedo boat weaving to seaward, bearing 145°, 5 nautical miles. Started attack.
1724 hours - In position 41°07'N, 16°57'E fired three torpedoes from 1500 yards. The torpedo boat was coming straight at Unseen from astern.
1725 hours - A torpedo explosion was heard.
1726 hours - A torpedo was heard to explode on hitting the beach.
1730 hours - Depth charging started. A total of 23 were dropped, the first 6 were very close but no damage was sustained.
1830 hours - Returned to periscope depth. The torpedo boat was seen to be hunting to the westward. The target was seen to enter Bari. (8)

27 Aug 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Rastello (985 GRT, former Greek Messarya Nomikos, built 1895) 48 nautical miles in position 41°30'N, 17°27'E. The target was proceeding unescorted from Durazzo to Bari and four crew members were killed.
(All times are zone -2) 0740 hours - Sighted masts of a merchant vessel bearing 129°, 8 nautical miles. Started attack.
0852 hours - In position 41°30'N, 17°27'E fired 4 torpedoes. One hit was obtained.
0859 hours - Heard two explosions thought to be depth charges.
0930 hours - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight except wreckage and survivors on a raft.

1045 hours - Surfaced and went alongside the raft with 12 survivors on it. Two destroyers / torpedo boats were seen to be escorting but after the sinking the made off to Bari. Passed over food and morphine tablets for the three badly wounded and took on board four survivors. One was a Yugo-Slav which was very keen to come.
1115 hours - Withdrew to the north-east.
1130 hours - Dived. (8)

28 Aug 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary patrol vessel (sailing vessel) V 216 / Fabiola (103 GRT) with gunfire and scuttling charges 3 nautical miles bearing 250° of Saseno Island, Albania. The corvette Pomona was sent to hunt the submarine but failed to locate it.
(All times are zone -2) 0620 hours - In position 40°30'N, 19°05'E sighted masts of a schooner bearing 110°, range 5 nautical miles. Closed to investigate.

0715 hours - Surfaced on the port quarter of the target. Opened up with the Vickers gun and manned the 3" gun. The 3" failed to fire. The crew of the schooner nontheless abandoned ship, some jumping over the side while others lowered a boat.

Went alongside and sent over the boarding party. They found the Captain and Boatswain still on board. They had been deserted by their crew and were taken prisoner. After collecting any books and documents placed demolition charges.
0750 hours - Shore batteries opened fire.
0755 hours - Withdrew to seaward and shortly afterwards dived. (8)
1 Sep 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

15 Sep 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Italy between 43.00'N and 43.25'N and off Capraia Island.

She made the passage through the Tunisian War Channel together with HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) and ORP Dzik (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski). They were escorted by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).

17 Sep 1943 Around 1330 B/17, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) parted company with HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).
HMS BYMS 2028 was to return to Malta.

The submarines then proceeded independently to their patrol areas routed as follows; They were to coss latitude 39°01'N in longitudes as follows: HMS Unseen in 10°15' at 0630 B/18. HMS Ultor in 10°43'E at 1200 B/18. ORP Dzik in 10°27'E at 2200 B/18. HMS Uproar in 11°01'E at 0001 B/19. All four submarines were then to proceed on course 360° as far as latitude 42°N. Futher orders would be signalled. (25)

17 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1004 B/17, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN);

Onward routeing my signal timed 1400 B/16 refers. Tactic to cross latitude 42°N. HMS Unseen in 10°15'E about 0230 B/20 thence to zone 'SR' north of 43°N. HMS Ultor in 10°43'E about 0430 B/20 thence to zone 'SQ' north of 043°N. ORP Dzik in 10°27'E about 1430 B/20 thence to zone 'SQ' south of 43°N. HMS Uproar in 11°01'E about 2130 B/20 thence zone 'SR' south of 43°N. Submarines may withdraw from zone if necessary as follows; HMS Unseen into zone 'SP'. HMS Ultor into zone 'SP' or to westward of zone 'SQ'. ORP Dzik and HMS Uproar to the south of 42°30'N. Submerged bombing restrictions are permanently in force east of 08°E and north of 42°N. (26)

21 Sep 1943 HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) torpedoed and sank the German minelayer Brandenburg (3895 GRT, former French Kita, built 1937) and the German night fighter direction vessel Kreta (2600 GRT, former French Ile de Beaute, built 1930) about 7 nautical miles north-east of Isola di Capraia, Italy in position 43°08'N, 09°58'E. They were escorted by the minesweepers R 189 and R 201 and two Arado seaplanes. Both vessels were each hit by a torpedo. Brandenburg sank at 1647 and Kreta at 1740 hours. Twenty-five were reported missing and thirty wounded (seven seriously) from Brandenburg and five were killed or missing and eleven wounded (four seriously) from Kreta. A total of 486 survivors were picked up by the minesweepers and brought to Leghorn.

(All times are zone -2) 1603 hours - In position 43°08'N, 09°58'E sighted two merchant vessels bearing 058°, range 5 nautical miles. Started attack. Two E-boats were seen as escort and two aircraft were patrolling overhead.
1623 hours - Fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards at the overlapping targets. Went deep on firing.
1626 hours - Heard two hits. Only HE from the E-boats was heard from now on.
